prompt: Why does the ocean look blue?
output: Water is clear but it absorbs light as it goes deeper. It doesn't absorb all wavelengths of light equally though; it absorbs reds more strongly (which is why water appears greenish when it's deep). The result is that if you have a big body of water then at first glance it will appear clear but as you go further down there won't be much visible because the amount of light gets less and less. This means that we see more of the surface area compared to the rest of the water so our brain assumes that the whole thing must be blue since that's what we're seeing most of.

prompt: Can you explain how vaccines work?
output:
Vaccines contain a small amount of something that causes disease. The immune system fights it off by creating antibodies against it. Then if you get infected with the same pathogen again, those antibodies will recognize it as foreign and fight it before you become sick. Vaccines also cause memory cells which remember what antigens they have seen before so when the body encounters them again it can mount a faster response.

This is why getting vaccinated makes you less likely to contract diseases such as chicken pox, flu, polio, etc. It does NOT make you immune however; sometimes people who have been vaccinated still catch these diseases but do not get very sick because their bodies already know about the virus.

prompt: What causes the seasons to change?
output: The Earth is tilted 23 degrees from its orbit around the sun. This means that during part of the year (winter) one hemisphere will always face away from the sun, while the other (summer) faces towards it. The same thing happens on Mars, but since they have a much longer orbital period, their winters/summers last twice as long.

prompt: Explain what compound interest is and why it matters.
output: Let's say I give you $100 right now, and then every year after that for 35 years I put another dollar into your piggy bank (so you have a total of 36 dollars). Then I come back in 37 years time and ask you if you want me to pay you all the money from the piggybank OR I can just give you a penny and double it each day until the end of those 38 years? If you wait long enough, doubling pennies will outpace any amount of money you could ever save up. This is because compounding works exponentially: Each new deposit earns as much interest as everything before it. So even though we only add one more dollar per year, our deposits grow at an increasing rate over time. That means our last deposit has earned many times more interest than our first one did. The same principle applies when banks charge us interest on loans: We don't get charged anything for the first dollar we borrow, but by the time we've borrowed millions, we're paying lots of interest for every extra dollar we take out.

It is caused by spastic contractions of the diaphragm muscle that causes a small amount of air to go into the stomach through the esophagus causing it to expand slightly which irritates some nerves that also control the spasms causing them to stop. The reason they occur most often when you're full after eating is because food takes up more room in your abdomen so there's less room for your stomach to expand before irritating those nerves. Hiccups can happen any time but usually last only seconds at a time. If they don't subside on their own within 24 hours then you should see a doctor about it. There may be something wrong if they keep happening repeatedly over long periods of time.

prompt: How does the internet send a message from my computer to another one?
output:
The Internet is made up of many different computers connected together by cables (or sometimes satellites) These computers all speak a common language called TCP/IP which allows them to talk to each other.

So when I want to send a piece of information (a webpage for example) to someone else's computer I need to find out where they live on the Internet so that I can tell my computer how to get there. This is done through DNS which stands for Domain Name System

Once both computers know where eachother are then they can start talking to eachother directly using IP packets. Each packet contains some data as well as its address and the address of the destination computer. 

This process repeats until every part of the original message has been received at the destination machine

prompt: Why does bread rise when you bake it?
output: When yeast is exposed to warm water and sugar (or flour) they begin to multiply rapidly by budding, also releasing carbon dioxide gas as waste. The CO2 gets trapped in the dough because of all the starches and sugars present which acts as a sort of net trapping the gas molecules. This causes the dough to expand until eventually it bursts and the bread falls out of its container onto the baking tray where it then hardens into a loaf. Bread without yeast will not do this process so won't rise at all.

prompt: What is the difference between weather and climate?
output: Weather refers to short term atmospheric conditions such as rain, snow, wind, temperature changes, etc... Climate is how these variables change over longer periods of time (months/years). A place might have warm summers but cold winters, so their climate would be described as having four seasons. But if it rained one day and then was sunny the next that's just changing weather. Weather can also be local where climate is more general. If I had a nice day outside today, that doesn't mean my city has good weather for all year round.

They play back the opposite sound wave of whatever is coming at them. If there's some annoying 50hz tone playing on your speaker system, it will make the air molecules around it vibrate up and down really fast (50 times per second). Your ear hears that because when those vibrating molecules hit your eardrum they cause it to move up and then down again. The headphones have little speakers built into them too but instead of making loud noises, they produce very quiet sounds. They take what they hear from the outside world and create a sound exactly opposite to it so if you're hearing something that makes your eardsrump go up 1cm, your headphones make the eardrums go down 1 cm as well. Together these two effects cancel each other out completely leaving no vibrations for your ears to pick up!

prompt: Why does metal feel colder than wood at the same temperature?
output: Metals have higher thermal conductivity than woods. When you touch something it transfers heat from your body into itself until they reach equilibrium (same temp). Metals transfer that energy faster because of their molecular structure so you lose more heat per unit time compared to wood which has lower conductivity. This is why touching metal feels colder even though both are at the exact same temperature.

prompt: Can you explain what a black hole is without the heavy math?
output:
It's really hard to answer that question because it would take pages of explanation just to get to the point where I could say something meaningful about them.

But here's my best attempt:

Imagine two balls on a string tied together by their centers. One ball has mass m1, one has mass 2m1. Now imagine pulling these two balls apart so they're very far away from each other (and any other massive bodies). The gravitational force between them will be F = G*m1*2m / r^2

Now imagine shrinking those balls down until both have radii less than some distance d such that if you took one of them and put its center at the location of the other ball's center, the distance between their surfaces would still be equal to the original value r. That means that the radius of the first ball must now be smaller than the initial value, let's call it r1, which we can write as: r - d > r1 => r1 < r -d 

So now our equation becomes: F = GM/r'^2 * M/ (r' - d) 
Where r' is the new, smaller radius of our first ball.
We want to find out how small r' needs to be for us to stop being able to escape from the surface of the second ball, so we set up another equation:
GM/(r')^2 = v^2/r'

Then solve for v^3 = GM*r'
And plug that back into the previous expression for F:
F = v ^2 * r'/r'^2 =v^2 /r'

At this point we know that F>0 implies v^>0, but since the speed needed to escape is greater than zero then the net force acting on us when we reach the second body is also positive, i.e., there is still a net outward force on us, so gravity isn't strong enough yet. We need to keep making the size of the object even smaller! If we do this long enough eventually the solution for v will become imaginary, meaning that no amount of energy could ever give us a speed fast enough to escape, and therefore the net gravitational pull is stronger than anything else going on. This critical size is called the Schwarzschild Radius.

prompt: Why do onions make you cry when you cut them?
output: Because they release a gas that irritates your eyes (and nose) causing tears. This is why it's best to chop onions away from your face while standing somewhere ventilated. Also, because the onion cells have been ruptured by cutting, the enzymes inside of the cell come into contact with the sugars within the cell. These two substances react together forming sulfenic acid which then reacts further to form propanethial sulfoxide - the irritating gas mentioned above.

prompt: My textbook says humans only use 10% of their brains. Is that true?
output:
Nope.

That is a myth which has been debunked by science for decades now (though it seems to have persisted among some members of the public). There's nothing special about the other 90%. It just isn't used as much as the parts we do use - but even those parts are still more active than they would otherwise be if all were truly unused. 

It might be worth pointing out that there are certain diseases such as Huntington's disease where brain tissue atrophies away. This can leave gaps in the cortex which can then allow surrounding cells to grow into the vacated space. The result is sometimes called 'paracortex' which sounds a lot like extra cortex. But it is simply not the case that our brains are designed with 9/10ths of them being unused!

prompt: A friend told me lightning never strikes the same place twice. Is that accurate?
output:
Lightning is attracted to the path of least resistance - meaning it will choose whatever route requires the least amount of energy for it to travel from point A (cloud) to point B (ground). That can mean hitting a building multiple times, or if there's no structure around it may just hit one tree over and over again. The reason we don't get struck by lightning more often is because our bodies aren't conductors so it doesn't want to go through us.

I've heard people say that when they were kids they used to play in puddles during thunderstorms because they believed that water would absorb the electricity and prevent them from getting shocked. They're wrong though. Water conducts electricity very well. If you happen to find yourself in a pool during a storm, stay away from metal fixtures and keep your feet as dry as possible. If the water is still moving then do *not* jump out into the rain. It might sound odd but the ground could actually have a charge on it at that moment due to the discharge. 

Also, don't touch any metal objects while inside a vehicle during a lightning storm. Even if the car has rubber tires, they won't provide enough insulation between you and the outside world. This includes touching the door handles. Wait until after the strike before opening the door.

prompt: Here's the opening of my college essay: "Ever since I was young, I have always loved science because it is very interesting and fun." How can I make it stronger?
output:
The first sentence shows that the writer has some knowledge about science but does not reveal much more information. The second sentence adds little value to the prompt as well; we already know that the subject is interesting and enjoyable.

What makes for a good hook?

A great hook should show us who the student really is instead of just telling us what they do. We need to see how their interests connect them to bigger ideas and themes beyond just the activity itself. What kind of impact did the student make through these activities? And why does it matter?

prompt: Make the case that public libraries are still essential in the digital age.
output: I think one of the most important functions is providing access for people who don't have it at home. A lot of jobs require internet use now. For example, I know someone whose job requires her to look up information on the internet daily but she doesn't have internet access at home because she can't afford to pay for it. She uses the library's computers every day before work so she can do her job. Libraries also provide free computer classes which many older adults attend if they're trying to learn how to navigate the internet. If they didn't have that option then some would probably give up entirely. Lastly, libraries offer a place where people from all walks of life come together to read, relax, study, etc. This promotes socialization among different groups of people. It allows us to see each other as individuals instead of just seeing each other based on our appearance.
